does: This paper introduces SAGE, an agent-based framework that interprets features discovered by sparse autoencoders in language models through an iterative, experimental process. Rather than generating single explanations passively, SAGE systematically formulates multiple candidate explanations for each SAE feature, designs targeted experiments to test these hypotheses, and refines explanations based on empirical activation patterns. The framework demonstrates significantly higher generative and predictive accuracy in explaining SAE features compared to existing baseline methods across diverse language model architectures.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using sparse autoencoders as interpretability tools for understanding learned representations in large language models. It extends prior work on automated feature interpretation and mechanistic interpretability by moving beyond single-pass explanation generation approaches toward iterative hypothesis testing and refinement. The paper connects to broader research on AI safety and transparency that motivates decomposing opaque model internals, while drawing on methodologies from scientific reasoning and experimental design that have recently been applied to understanding neural network components.
enables: SAGE's agentic interpretation framework sets a foundation for more rigorous and automated interpretability research at scale. By establishing an iterative experimental methodology for feature explanation, the work enables future systems to achieve deeper mechanistic understanding of language models with less manual oversight. This capability could support safer model deployment by identifying problematic learned features, facilitate debugging of model behaviors, and provide a scalable approach to validating whether SAE decompositions truly capture human-understandable concepts versus artifacts of the decomposition process.
